figures.caption: Figure 2: Architecture of the proposed mission-based DIL framework. The system coordinates continuous adaptation under strict storage constraints via a “Learn-and-Dispose” pipeline. (Top) Learnand-Dispose: Incoming mission data (Dtrain

Table 3: Comprehensive Comparison of Method Performance. We report both standard CL metrics (AP, BWT, FWT) and the proposed decoupled criteria (C1, C2, C3) under the standard forward sequence. Unlike AP which aggregates overall performance, C1–C3 rigorously isolate specific spatial generalization and temporal retention capabilities. Note that naive baselines exhibit severe negative BWT and catastrophic forgetting (low C3), verifying that high immediate plasticity (C2) cannot compensate for long-term stability.

Figure 5: Sensitivity Analysis of Buffer Capacity (B). (a) Retention (C3) and (b) Generalization (C1): Note that the y-axis denotes the final accuracy evaluated at step k = 10, corresponding to the strict definitions of C3 and C1. DBS dominates in the case of low buffer capacity (B ≤ 200). (c) Stability (BWT): DBS maintains positive backward transfer (knowledge consolidation) across all capacities, whereas Rand. suffers from forgetting (negative BWT) when storage is limited. (d) Performance & Efficiency (AP/FWT): Solid lines represent AP, while dashed lines represent FWT. DBS achieves peak AP and FWT at B = 200.

Table 4: Robustness Analysis across Different Mission Sequences (Standard Metrics). We also evaluate the stability of the methods under four distinct curriculum orders: Forward (VIS→IR), Backward (IR→VIS), Pressure (Hard Start), and Robust (Randomized). While LBS excels when hard missions appear early (Pressure/Robust), it experiences performance degradation in the Backward sequence. DBS demonstrates superior consistency, maintaining high AP and BWT across all scenarios.

Table 5: Robustness Analysis across Different Mission Sequences (Proposed Criteria). C1: Generalization. C2: Adaptation (Plasticity). C3: Retention (Stability). DBS exhibits high consistency across all orders, achieving the best balance. LBS degrades significantly in the Backward sequence due to the locking effect.

Table 6: Ablation Study on Allocation Strategies. Comparison under the heterogeneous memory framework (forward sequence). Min-Guar consistently achieves the highest stability (BWT) and retention (C3) by avoiding both the rigidity of Round-Robin and the starvation risk of Global allocation.

Table 1: Statistics of aerial mission sequences used in this work. Missions marked as “CL” form the lifelong learning sequence S, while “Unvisited” missions constitute the fixed generalization benchmark U .

Table 7: Computational Performance. Latency measures the selection strategy cost. FPS measures training throughput. With batched optimization, both utility-based strategies (LBS and DBS) incur minimal cost and maintain high throughput suitable for real-time deployment, outperforming distillation-based methods like DIL-iCaRL.

Figure 3: Evolution of Metrics. (Top) Generalization metric Acck(E(1)). DBS (Red/Diamond) maintains consistently competitive performance throughout the sequential steps. Note that DIL-iCaRL (Green/Star) exhibits slightly lower generalization in the final stages compared to DBS. k = 0 denotes the evaluation of the initial pre-trained model. (Bottom) Retention metric Acck(E(3) k ). DBS demonstrates superior stability, maintaining the highest accuracy trajectory and avoiding the sharp performance drops observed in baseline methods.

Figure 4: Visualization of Feature Space Coverage after the Lifelong Sequence (k = 10). The gray points represent the full feature manifold, while colored dots represent retained exemplars. (Left) Stochastic sampling exhibits sparse coverage due to stochastic decay, leaving large manifold areas empty. (Center) LBS tends to discard “easy” regions (low loss), resulting in structural gaps (e.g., the sparsely populated bottom-right manifold in LSZ-07). (Right) DBS achieves the most complete coverage. It retains a higher density of samples in complex regions (e.g., the top-left cluster in LSZ-07) while maintaining a complete structural skeleton in smoother regions.

Figure 1: Illustration of the substantial domain gaps in aerial VPR. The top row displays reference satellite imagery (satellite image tiles), while the bottom row shows the corresponding onboard aerial observations of the same geographic locations. Each column highlights specific distributional shifts encountered during missions: (from left to right) varying perspective & illumination, distinct modality (VIS vs. IR) & scale differences (induced by flight altitude and camera parameters), complex seasonal transitions and physical structure & surface evolution (e.g., structural modifications or land cover changes). Crucially, these variations represent shifting domains rather than visual disturbances. Static pre-trained models cannot encapsulate such unbounded operational and environmental variability, necessitating the study of an adaptive domain-incremental learning (DIL) framework.
